role_summary: President of the Forrest County, Mississippi chapter of the NAACP; killed January 10, 1966 in a Ku Klux Klan firebombing of his Hattiesburg home, the violence his wife Ellie Dahmer (catalog entry 36) and son Vernon Dahmer Jr. (catalog entry 121) survived.
ai_reading: Dahmer Sr. is the subject of both the Ellie Dahmer (entry 36) and Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121) catalog pages, where the January 10, 1966 firebombing is the anchor event. He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee (his murder predated the project by four decades); his standalone catalog page exists so his own name is findable alongside his survivors' pages and so the FBI Bowers-prosecution arc has a third anchor in the catalog. The eventual 1998 state-court conviction of Imperial Wizard Samuel Bowers for ordering the firebombing was the close of one of the longest-running cold-case prosecutions of the civil rights era; the Dahmer family interviewees (Ellie and Vernon Jr) speak to that thirty-two-year wait.
biographical_paragraph: Dahmer was born March 10, 1908 in Forrest County, Mississippi and built a successful livelihood as a farmer, sawmill operator, grocer, and cotton ginner outside Hattiesburg. He served as president of the Forrest County NAACP and, beginning in 1962, allowed African American voters to pay their poll taxes at his grocery store, a service his wife Ellie noted he announced publicly in the days before his death. On the night of January 10, 1966 Ku Klux Klansmen firebombed his home; Dahmer returned fire from inside as his wife and children escaped through a window, but he was overcome by burns and smoke inhalation and died the following day at the Hattiesburg hospital. The killers were members of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan, the Mississippi faction whose Imperial Wizard Samuel Bowers had also ordered the 1964 Mississippi Burning murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner. Federal civil rights charges produced four convictions in 1968; state murder charges against Bowers were retried four times before a 1998 conviction. Dahmer is buried in the family cemetery in the Kelly Settlement community in Forrest County.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ellie Dahmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: His widow Ellie Dahmer (entry 36), asked what her husband was like, describes the household their family kept outside Hattiesburg before the firebombing:
interview_snippets.quote: If I've ever seen a Christian person he was one. He put Christianity into practice. He was really concerned about his fellowman. We had a comfortable living on the farm in the sawmill and the grocery store on the plane of mail. At that time we had a very comfortable living. But he wanted to make things better for other people.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ellie Dahmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: Ellie Dahmer (entry 36) answers whether her husband was himself registered to vote at the time he was killed:
interview_snippets.quote: No, he never had a chance to register. He never had a chance to vote. His voter registration card came [back from the Justice Department] in time to be buried with him.

interview_snippets.speaker: Vernon Dahmer Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: His son Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121) recounts how his father helped charter the local NAACP branch in the mid-1940s:
interview_snippets.quote: So when in the mid 1940s, my dad had heard about the NAACP started up, OK? And he and eight other black men established the farest-counter branch of the NAACP. It was nine of them that started it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Vernon Dahmer Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121), stationed at March Air Force Base in California, recalls the morning he learned of the attack:
interview_snippets.quote: Monday morning, January 10, 1966, I received a telephone call from one of my dad's sisters who lived in Compton [then] South Central LA, Compton, California. And I was informed of what had happened. The house had been fire bombed.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: Hattiesburg NAACP figure Clarence Magee (entry 21), asked whether he knew Dahmer, places him through his schoolmate sons:
interview_snippets.quote: I knew Vernon Dahmer, but I knew him primarily through his sons. Two of his sons were class mates with me at all.

interview_snippets.speaker: Raylawni G. Branch
interview_snippets.lead_in: Raylawni G. Branch (entry 100), recalling the Hattiesburg years before she left for nursing school in New York, marks the date of the killing:
interview_snippets.quote: I left here in 1966, Mr. [Vernon] Dahmer died January 10th, 1966.

gallery.alt: Mourners at the funeral of Vernon Dahmer Sr., Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 1966
gallery.caption: The funeral of Vernon Dahmer Sr. in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 1966, days after the January 10 Ku Klux Klan firebombing of his home. Photograph from the Moncrief Photograph Collection of the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
